quizzes.title: Ch 9 Quiz
quizzes.description: Ch. 9 Quiz mapped from the completed OTS.5110.ONL-2026SP attempt for March 30 - April 5.

questions.question: The title of "Deuteronomy" is associated with "second law."
questions.explanation: The chapter explains that Deuteronomy comes from a title meaning "second law," while also noting that the book is more than a simple second copy of earlier laws.

questions.question: The narrative setting of the book is on top of Mount Sinai.
questions.explanation: Deuteronomy is set on the plains of Moab across the Jordan from Jericho, as Israel prepares to enter Canaan, not on Mount Sinai.

questions.question: The narrative framework of the book is Moses' farewell address to the Israelites.
questions.explanation: The book is framed as Moses addressing Israel near the end of his life, reviewing the covenant and calling the new generation to faithfulness.

questions.question: Monotheism is the belief in the oneness and consistency of God.
questions.explanation: The chapter connects Israelite monotheism with the confession that Yahweh is one, emphasizing his uniqueness, unity, and consistent covenant character.

questions.question: The Shema is in Exodus 19:4.
questions.explanation: The Shema belongs to Deuteronomy 6:4, where Israel is called to hear that the Lord is one; Exodus 19:4 is not its location.

questions.question: Deuteronomy 28 describes the blessings and curses of the covenant.
questions.explanation: Deuteronomy 28 presents covenant blessings for obedience and curses for disobedience, forming a major basis for later retribution theology.

questions.question: Deuteronomic theology can be described as a theology of ecclesiology.
questions.explanation: The chapter describes Deuteronomic theology in terms of covenant obedience, blessing, curse, and retribution, not ecclesiology.

questions.question: The suzerainty treaties were political covenants between unequal partners.
questions.explanation: Ancient suzerainty treaties were covenants between unequal parties, with a stronger ruler setting covenant obligations for a vassal.

questions.question: Joshua has a positive view of the Israelites' obedience at the end of the book.
questions.explanation: Joshua ends with covenant warnings and the need for continuing obedience; in the Deuteronomistic framework, Israel is not given an unqualified positive evaluation.

questions.question: The Deuteronomistic History consists of Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy.
questions.explanation: The Deuteronomistic History refers to the historical narrative associated with Deuteronomy through Kings, not the five books from Genesis through Deuteronomy.

quizzes.title: Ch 10 Quiz
quizzes.description: Ch. 10 Quiz mapped from the completed OTS.5110.ONL-2026SP attempt for April 6 - April 12.

questions.question: The "Historical Books" are important in part because of their historical content.
questions.explanation: The chapter stresses that the Historical Books matter because they preserve the story of Israel in the land across many centuries.

questions.question: These books describe Israel's history, but they are more than history or a mere record of historical facts.
questions.explanation: The Historical Books are theological history: they report events, but they also interpret those events as God's word to his people.

questions.question: The books Isaiah, Ezekiel, Jeremiah, and The Twelve are known as the Former Prophets.
questions.explanation: In the Hebrew canon, Joshua, Judges, Samuel, and Kings are the Former Prophets; Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and the Twelve are Latter Prophets.

questions.question: Heilsgeschichte is a German term that denotes "salvation history."
questions.explanation: The chapter defines Heilsgeschichte as "salvation history," a way of reading Israel's story as part of God's saving work.

questions.question: Judges is a happy book detailing Israel's single-minded devotion to God.
questions.explanation: Judges portrays moral and spiritual decline in Israel, not a happy period of single-minded devotion to God.

questions.question: All of the historical books are authored by the same person after whom the book is titled.
questions.explanation: The chapter notes that the Historical Books are generally anonymous and that their titles identify subject matter or major figures, not necessarily authors.

questions.question: The classic expression of the Deuteronomistic History was given by Martin Noth.
questions.explanation: Martin Noth gave the classic modern statement of the Deuteronomistic History, especially the idea of a unified history shaped by Deuteronomic theology.

questions.question: The notion of a Deuteronomistic History is sometimes used to describe a single composition from Deuteronomy through Kings in the Hebrew order.
questions.explanation: The term Deuteronomistic History is often used for the composition running from Deuteronomy through Kings in the Hebrew ordering.

questions.question: Acknowledging the overarching philosophy of retribution theology in history brings a certain unity to the historical books.
questions.explanation: Retribution theology gives unity to the Historical Books by explaining Israel's history through covenant faithfulness, disobedience, blessing, and judgment.

questions.question: Chronicles, Ezra-Nehemiah, Ruth, and Esther are not in the same arranged order in the Hebrew order of the Bible as they are in the English order of the Bible.
questions.explanation: Chronicles, Ezra-Nehemiah, Ruth, and Esther appear in different canonical locations in the Hebrew Bible than in the English Old Testament.

quizzes.title: Ch 11-12 Quiz
quizzes.description: Ch. 11-12 Quiz mapped from the completed OTS.5110.ONL-2026SP attempt for April 13 - April 19.

questions.question: The outline for the book of Joshua was Israel Conquers the Land (1-12), Israel Divides the Land (13-21), and Israel Begins to Settle into the Land (22-24).
questions.explanation: The Joshua chapter outlines the book around conquest of the land, division of the land, and Israel beginning to settle in the land.

questions.question: Joshua 24:13 indicates that Joshua is the author when it says, "I, Joshua, write this greeting in my own hand."
questions.explanation: Joshua 24:13 does not contain that authorship statement; the chapter instead discusses authorship more cautiously, including Joshua 24:26 as evidence Joshua wrote some material.

questions.question: The mention of Jebusites in Jerusalem and Canaanites in Gezer suggest that the book was in its present form no later than Solomon's time.
questions.explanation: The references to Jebusites in Jerusalem and Canaanites in Gezer fit a setting before Solomon fully changed those conditions, so the present form is no later than Solomon's time.

questions.question: The Davidic covenant is a theme of the book of Joshua according to the textbook.
questions.explanation: Joshua emphasizes transition from Moses to Joshua, conquest and division of the land, and God's faithfulness to his promises; the Davidic covenant is not listed as a Joshua theme.

questions.question: The expression "in those days Israel had no king" suggests the author's perspective was from the later vantage point of the monarchy.
questions.explanation: The repeated note that Israel had no king points to an author looking back from the period of monarchy.

questions.question: The judges of the Bible act precisely as our contemporary judges act in America.
questions.explanation: Biblical judges were charismatic deliverers and leaders raised up in crisis, not courtroom officials equivalent to modern American judges.

questions.question: The author of the book of Judges used a cycle of sin, war as judgment, repentance, and deliverance in order to introduce certain judges.
questions.explanation: Judges repeatedly introduces deliverers through a pattern of Israel's sin, divine judgment through oppression or war, repentance, and deliverance.

questions.question: Historical problems in the book of Judges include chronology and political structure.
questions.explanation: The chapter identifies chronology and political structure as two major historical problems in interpreting Judges.

questions.question: Scholars have concluded that an amphictyony describes exactly what was occurring in the days of the judges.
questions.explanation: The chapter treats amphictyony as a scholarly proposal for Israel's tribal organization, not as something proven to describe exactly the period of the judges.

questions.question: I read all of I Samuel.
questions.explanation: This item functions as a reading-attestation question for the course assignment rather than a claim from the textbook chapter.

quizzes.title: Ch 13-14 Quiz
quizzes.description: Ch. 13-14 Quiz mapped from the completed OTS.5110.ONL-2026SP attempt for April 20 - April 26.

questions.question: Saul was successful as king because he obeyed the commands of God.
questions.explanation: First Samuel presents Saul as rejected because of disobedience to God, so his kingship is not described as successful on the basis of obedience.

questions.question: The textbook lists David's rise to prominence as a theme in 1 Samuel.
questions.explanation: The chapter lists David's rise to prominence as one of the major themes of 1 Samuel.

questions.question: The textbook indicates that the sentence in 1 Sam 27:6, "Zigklag has belonged to the kings of Judah to this day" suggests some time had elapsed since the division of the kingdom.
questions.explanation: The note that Ziklag belonged to the kings of Judah suggests the writer is looking back after the kingdom had divided and time had passed.

questions.question: The book of 1 Samuel reports that Samuel wrote the book.
questions.explanation: The chapter treats 1 Samuel as anonymous; the book does not report that Samuel wrote it.

questions.question: God looked at David's family lineage in order to choose him.
questions.explanation: David is chosen because the Lord looks at the heart rather than outward appearance or family status.

questions.question: The textbook lists God's covenant with David as a theme in 2 Samuel.
questions.explanation: The chapter identifies God's covenant with David as a central theme of 2 Samuel.

questions.question: Jerusalem was Israel's spiritual center as well as its political center.
questions.explanation: David made Jerusalem the political center by capturing it and the spiritual center by bringing the ark there.

questions.question: David wanted to build God a house, but God told David he would build him a "house" of descendants.
questions.explanation: In 2 Samuel 7, David wants to build a temple-house for God, but God promises to build David a house, meaning a dynasty of descendants.

questions.question: The word "covenant" is not used in 2 Samuel 7.
questions.explanation: Although later biblical texts describe the promise as a covenant, the actual word "covenant" is not used in 2 Samuel 7.

questions.question: God's "covenant" with David included a provision to include Saul's son in the dynasty.
questions.explanation: The Davidic covenant concerns David's own line and the rule of his descendants; it does not provide for Saul's son to continue the dynasty.

quizzes.title: Ch 15-16 Quiz
quizzes.description: Ch. 15-16 Quiz mapped from the completed OTS.5110.ONL-2026SP attempt.

questions.question: The textbook indicates that retribution theology plays no role in the writing of 1 Kings.
questions.explanation: The 1 Kings chapter says retribution theology is central to Kings, evaluating kings and nations by covenant obedience and disobedience.

questions.question: The textbook indicates that a consideration of how the author composed the book is unimportant.
questions.explanation: The chapter treats the author's composition as important, including sources, regnal formulas, and theological evaluation of each king.

questions.question: The perspective of the author of the book is from someone prior to the fall of the northern kingdom.
questions.explanation: Kings is written from the perspective of someone who knows the fall of both kingdoms, especially the exile after Judah's fall, not from before northern Israel fell.

questions.question: Solomon worshiped God alone all the days of his life.
questions.explanation: Solomon did not remain faithful to worshiping God alone; 1 Kings explains that his foreign wives turned his heart toward other gods.

questions.question: Out of a struggle concerning Baalism, the office of prophecy began to emerge based on the ministries of Elijah and Elisha.
questions.explanation: The chapter connects the struggle against Baalism with the prominent prophetic ministries of Elijah and Elisha.

questions.question: The textbook lists the "history of Israel from the viewpoint of the Mosaic covenant" as a theme of Kings.
questions.explanation: The 2 Kings chapter lists Israel's history from the viewpoint of the Mosaic covenant as a major theological theme of Kings.

questions.question: The textbook lists the "the role and significance of prophecy" as a theme of Kings.
questions.explanation: The same chapter highlights prophecy as a major theme, showing how God's word confronts kings and is fulfilled in history.

questions.question: Israel was destroyed because the people broke God's statutes and commandments, followed worthless idols, and became worthless themselves.
questions.explanation: Second Kings 17 explains Israel's destruction in covenant terms: the people broke God's statutes and commandments and followed worthless idols.

questions.question: Elisha's ministry helped build up the dynasty of Omri.
questions.explanation: Elisha's ministry helped bring about the destruction of the Omri dynasty, rather than building it up.

questions.question: The book of II Kings ends on a hopeful note concerning the Davidic monarchy.
questions.explanation: Second Kings ends with Jehoiachin released from prison, leaving a son of David alive and giving a hopeful note about the Davidic line.
